scenario:
Situation: Line 3 down at 22:47. Need 500 units of bushing P/N B-447 by 06:00.
Without parts, we miss a $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 tomorrow.

Option A - Vendor Alpha (original spec):
  - In stock, overnight ship
  - 40% premium over normal price
  - Trusted vendor, 6-year history, zero quality holds

Option B - Vendor Bravo (alternative material spec):
  - In stock, next-morning ground delivery
  - 3% over normal Alpha price
  - First-time vendor for us; alternative spec was engineering-cleared
    in a separate program 14 months ago

Engineering is unreachable until 07:00.

Line context: Vendor Bravo's alt-spec material was run on THIS Line 3 just last month (within 90 days) to cover a gap. It ran with zero quality holds — but informally: the run was never formally engineering-cleared for this line, and there is no signed clearance on file.

decision.rationale: Vendor Alpha carries a 40% cost premium, which exceeds the 20% escalation threshold and therefore cannot be approved unilaterally regardless of the trusted relationship or delivery fit. Vendor Bravo's alt-spec material did run on Line 3 within 90 days without a quality hold, which would normally satisfy the same-line recent history heuristic — but the scenario explicitly states that run was never formally engineering-cleared for this line and no signed clearance exists on file. Under Identity rule #1, spec adherence is non-negotiable unless engineering says otherwise *in writing, for this line, recently*; that written clearance is absent here. The 2025-06-14 lesson learned also reinforces that under-pressure switches without a same-shift engineering signoff led to a $180k recall, so I will not repeat that pattern. Both options independently trigger escalation criteria, and engineering is reachable at 07:00 — before the 14:00 ship commitment — making a brief hold viable rather than catastrophic.
identity_clauses_invoked: Never approve cost premiums above 20% without escalation.
Spec adherence is non-negotiable unless engineering says otherwise in writing, for this line, recently.
Prefer original-spec material unless an alternative has explicit, recent (≤90 days) engineering clearance for THIS line.

Same-line recent history rule (added 2025-11-02): if the alternative material has been run on the SAME line within the last 90 days without a quality hold, treat that as engineering-cleared. [NOT MET — no formal clearance on file, run was informal]
When uncertain, pause and escalate. Downtime is recoverable; quality escapes on a customer order are not.
2025-06-14 lesson: under-pressure switches need a same-shift engineering signoff, not a phone-call deferral.
Any cost premium > 20%, OR any spec deviation without recent clearance, OR any first-time vendor on a customer-deadline-driven order [triggers escalation].
